context:
Section 4.2 (Nonuniform PS-monotonicity), page 13 (around Proposition 4.5 discussion).

The construction used to show Proposition 4.5 is already considerably complicated when \(m = 3\); proving the claim for all (or a broad class of) voting rules when \(m \ge 4\) remains an interesting problem.
title: Extend nonuniform public-spirit distortion monotonicity to four or more alternatives
background_latex:
Consider an election with a finite set of voters \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\) and a finite set of alternatives \([m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\). Each voter \(i\) has a utility function \(u_i:[m]\to[0,1]\); equivalently, utilities form a matrix \(U\in[0,1]^{n\times m}\) with entries \(U(i,a)=u_i(a)\). The utilitarian social welfare of an alternative \(a\in[m]\) is
\[\mathrm{sw}(a,U)=\sum_{i=1}^n u_i(a).\]
Each voter has a \emph{public spirit} level \(\gamma_i\in[0,1]\), collected in a vector \(\gamma\in[0,1]^n\). Given \((U,\gamma)\), define voter \(i\)'s public-spirited value (PS-value) for alternative \(a\) by
\[v_i(a;U,\gamma)=(1-\gamma_i)u_i(a)+\gamma_i\,\frac{\mathrm{sw}(a,U)}{n}.\]
Voters submit strict rankings consistent with decreasing PS-values: for alternatives \(a,b\), if \(v_i(a;U,\gamma)>v_i(b;U,\gamma)\) then voter \(i\) ranks \(a\) above \(b\); if equal, either order is allowed. Let \(\Pi(U,\gamma)\) denote the set of all strict preference profiles (one ranking per voter) consistent with \((U,\gamma)\) in this sense.
A deterministic (resolute) voting rule is a function \(f\) mapping each strict preference profile \(\pi\) to a winning alternative \(f(\pi)\in[m]\).
For a fixed instance \((U,\gamma)\) and a profile \(\pi\in\Pi(U,\gamma)\), define the (utilitarian) distortion of \(f\) on \((U,\gamma,\pi)\) as
\[\mathrm{dist}(f;U,\gamma,\pi)=\frac{\max_{a\in[m]}\mathrm{sw}(a,U)}{\mathrm{sw}(f(\pi),U)},\]
with the convention that if \(\mathrm{sw}(f(\pi),U)=0\) and the numerator is positive then the ratio is \(+\infty\).
The worst-case distortion of \(f\) under PS-vector \(\gamma\) is
\[\mathrm{dist}(f,\gamma)=\sup_{U\in[0,1]^{n\times m}}\;\sup_{\pi\in\Pi(U,\gamma)}\mathrm{dist}(f;U,\gamma,\pi).\]
For two PS-vectors \(\gamma,\gamma'\in[0,1]^n\), write \(\gamma'\ge \gamma\) if \(\gamma'_i\ge \gamma_i\) for all voters \(i\).
problem_definition_latex:
Fix \(m\ge 4\). Determine whether the following monotonicity inequality holds for \emph{all} deterministic voting rules \(f\): for every number of voters \(n\) and every pair of PS-vectors \(\gamma,\gamma'\in[0,1]^n\) with \(\gamma'\ge \gamma\),
\[\mathrm{dist}(f,\gamma')\le \mathrm{dist}(f,\gamma).\]
Equivalently, decide whether increasing voters' public spirit levels componentwise can ever increase the worst-case utilitarian distortion when \(m\ge 4\).
latex:
Consider an election with a finite set of voters \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\) and a finite set of alternatives \([m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\). Each voter \(i\) has a utility function \(u_i:[m]\to[0,1]\); equivalently, utilities form a matrix \(U\in[0,1]^{n\times m}\) with entries \(U(i,a)=u_i(a)\). The utilitarian social welfare of an alternative \(a\in[m]\) is
\[\mathrm{sw}(a,U)=\sum_{i=1}^n u_i(a).\]
Each voter has a \emph{public spirit} level \(\gamma_i\in[0,1]\), collected in a vector \(\gamma\in[0,1]^n\). Given \((U,\gamma)\), define voter \(i\)'s public-spirited value (PS-value) for alternative \(a\) by
\[v_i(a;U,\gamma)=(1-\gamma_i)u_i(a)+\gamma_i\,\frac{\mathrm{sw}(a,U)}{n}.\]
Voters submit strict rankings consistent with decreasing PS-values: for alternatives \(a,b\), if \(v_i(a;U,\gamma)>v_i(b;U,\gamma)\) then voter \(i\) ranks \(a\) above \(b\); if equal, either order is allowed. Let \(\Pi(U,\gamma)\) denote the set of all strict preference profiles (one ranking per voter) consistent with \((U,\gamma)\) in this sense.
A deterministic (resolute) voting rule is a function \(f\) mapping each strict preference profile \(\pi\) to a winning alternative \(f(\pi)\in[m]\).
For a fixed instance \((U,\gamma)\) and a profile \(\pi\in\Pi(U,\gamma)\), define the (utilitarian) distortion of \(f\) on \((U,\gamma,\pi)\) as
\[\mathrm{dist}(f;U,\gamma,\pi)=\frac{\max_{a\in[m]}\mathrm{sw}(a,U)}{\mathrm{sw}(f(\pi),U)},\]
with the convention that if \(\mathrm{sw}(f(\pi),U)=0\) and the numerator is positive then the ratio is \(+\infty\).
The worst-case distortion of \(f\) under PS-vector \(\gamma\) is
\[\mathrm{dist}(f,\gamma)=\sup_{U\in[0,1]^{n\times m}}\;\sup_{\pi\in\Pi(U,\gamma)}\mathrm{dist}(f;U,\gamma,\pi).\]
For two PS-vectors \(\gamma,\gamma'\in[0,1]^n\), write \(\gamma'\ge \gamma\) if \(\gamma'_i\ge \gamma_i\) for all voters \(i\).

Fix \(m\ge 4\). Determine whether the following monotonicity inequality holds for \emph{all} deterministic voting rules \(f\): for every number of voters \(n\) and every pair of PS-vectors \(\gamma,\gamma'\in[0,1]^n\) with \(\gamma'\ge \gamma\),
\[\mathrm{dist}(f,\gamma')\le \mathrm{dist}(f,\gamma).\]
Equivalently, decide whether increasing voters' public spirit levels componentwise can ever increase the worst-case utilitarian distortion when \(m\ge 4\).
open_problem_quote: The construction used to show Proposition 4.5 is already considerably complicated when \(m = 3\); proving the claim for all (or a broad class of) voting rules when \(m \ge 4\) remains an interesting problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For a fixed number of voters and alternatives, one can search over utility matrices and PS-vectors and compute induced rankings and distortion, but quantifying worst-case distortion involves an outer supremum and tie-breaking over multiple compatible profiles, which complicates brute-force searches. Still, small-instance searches could plausibly find counterexamples to the monotonicity inequality for specific rules or suggest conjectures.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects include explicit voting rules (represented algorithmically) or specific counterexample instances \((U,\gamma,\gamma')\). A fitness signal can be based on computed distortion gaps \(\mathrm{dist}(f,\gamma')-\mathrm{dist}(f,\gamma)\) over sampled utilities/profiles, but accurately estimating worst-case distortion is computationally heavy and may require heuristic maximization over instances and tie-breakings.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis: No relevant Scholar-organic forward citations were available for this problem. The forward-citation review therefore cannot identify follow-up work that resolves or materially advances the problem.
